body_txt: You know the story . A bored little boy watching the sheep yells (falsely) that a wolf is coming. The townspeople come running, and he is greatly entertained. Later, he does it again. They fall for it again. Then an actual wolf appears, but this time, no matter how much the little boy screams , nobody comes to drive it off. The wolf kills or scatters the sheep, and the little boy learns a valuable lesson. Grimmer versions end with the wolf eating the boy as well. Or everyone . Moral of the story: no one believes a liar, even when they're telling the truth. This can be taken two ways: "Don't be a liar" or "Even liars can tell the truth sometimes." Less traditional possibilities are " If you're going to lie, keep it plausible ," "never tell the same lie twice," " people will eventually tune out false alarms ," or even "don't leave a known liar on sheep watch." In a related plot, the Kid Hero initially gets caught telling a relatively minor lie, or at least it appears that way, and then struggles to get anyone to believe them when they've discovered something truly serious. Fortunately, the kid is either able to find witnesses to support him or is able to prove his claim, even if he has to save the day on his own. Eventually, the kid's reputation is restored either by the authorities being impressed by his heroism or the people he helped coming forward to praise his character, with the moral of the story changing to the tune of "Telling the truth isn't only the right thing to do, but comes with better benefits than lying". Adult protagonists can run afoul of this too: nothing complicates Lieutenant Amavore's investigation like accusing the wrong suspect halfway into the story. His credibility will take a major hit, and he'll have even more difficulty doing the one thing that will save his reputation: solving the Sapolsky murder. In psychology and communication studies, the "Cry Wolf" Effect is a direct reference to the trope, referring to instances where people are less likely to believe information and/or warnings from individuals and organizations that have given them misleading or incorrect information in the past—even when their information is accurate. Older Than Feudalism , The Boy Who Cried Wolf is the Trope Namer from Aesop's Fables . Which also makes it one of The Oldest Ones in the Book . A frequently subverted Undead Horse Trope . Not to be confused with the Metal Gear Solid 4: Guns of the Patriots boss Crying Wolf. See also: This Is Not a Drill , Cassandra Truth , Not-So-Imaginary Friend , No Mere Windmill , Mistaken for an Imposter , Not Now, Kiddo . Contrast While Rome Burns , when things are actually going horribly wrong but everyone is in denial of it. In situations like that, if the character crying wolf turns out to be right, and people still refuse to believe him, then he is either an Ignored Expert or The Cassandra . Subtrope of Poor Communication Kills . When an intruder deliberately triggers seemingly-bogus alerts until people stop responding to them, it's False False Alarm . Not to be confused with a wolf that is crying . This trope provides examples of the following situations:  On a similar note, see No Permanence, No Stakes for when this situation extends to an author and their audience, where viewers become unable to take the work at face-value because of certain story decisions made by the writer.
